title: Easter Day
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## April 22, 2019 | Easter Day Sermon by Richard Lawson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

April 22, 2019 was Easter Sunday, the culmination of the Christian liturgical year and the most significant feast day in the church calendar. The lectionary readings for Easter Day (RCL) would have included Acts 10:34-43 (Peter's speech about the resurrection), Psalm 118:1-2, 14-24, 1 Corinthians 15:1-11 (Paul on the resurrection), and John 20:1-18 (Mary Magdalene at the empty tomb). This was the first Sunday of the Easter season, marking the end of the six-week Lenten period and the beginning of the 50-day celebration leading to Pentecost.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of General Convention 2018 (held in July 2018 in Austin, Texas), which had approved significant changes to marriage canon law in November 2018, allowing priests to decline to solemnize same-sex marriages based on religious belief while requiring dioceses to ensure access to marriage services. The Diocese of Colorado, which includes Saint John's Cathedral, was led by Bishop Robert J. Wright at this time, and the church was navigating the implementation of these canonical changes. No major bishop elections or diocesan crises occurred in the immediate weeks before Easter 2019.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical spring weather in late April, with warming temperatures after winter. The primary local focus was on the Colorado legislature, which was in session and debating bills related to education funding and economic issues. The Denver Nuggets (NBA) were in the midst of their playoff run (they would eventually lose in the second round), generating significant sports attention in the metro area. No major disasters or acute community crises affected the Capitol Hill or downtown Denver neighborhoods in the week immediately before Easter.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Mueller investigation had concluded on March 24, 2019, with Attorney General William Barr releasing his summary stating there was no evidence of criminal conspiracy between the Trump campaign and Russia, though the question of obstruction of justice remained contested; the full report would not be released until April 18, 2019, just days before Easter. The 2020 presidential campaign was in early stages, with Senator Bernie Sanders, former Vice President Joe Biden (who would officially announce on April 25), and other Democratic candidates competing for attention. Concerns about the border crisis and immigration policy dominated political discourse, with the Trump administration continuing hardline policies.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Notre-Dame Cathedral fire in Paris occurred on April 15, 2019, just one week before this Easter sermon, capturing global attention and raising questions about religious heritage and reconstruction; the fire would have been fresh in the minds of the Denver congregation. The Brexit crisis was ongoing in the United Kingdom, with the March 29 deadline having passed and negotiations continuing under Prime Minister Theresa May, creating uncertainty about Britain's departure from the European Union. The Syrian civil war continued with Russian and Iranian backing of Assad, and U.S. troops remained in Syria despite Trump's stated desire for withdrawal.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The cultural mood in late April 2019 was one of political polarization and deep partisan division, with the Mueller report's release having intensified debates about presidential accountability and the rule of law. The environmental movement was gaining momentum in the lead-up to Earth Day (April 22), and the Colorado congregation would have been aware of climate change discussions. Entertainment-wise, "Game of Thrones" was in its final season (the series finale aired May 19), and the Marvel Cinematic Universe was building toward "Avengers: Endgame" (released April 26), making it a dominant cultural conversation; the blockbuster release timing meant secular entertainment was competing for cultural attention during a major religious observance.
